paragraphs.text: Scott O'Dell of Stoneapple Farm drew from a life spent along the California coast instead of books for material for his 1960 Newberry [sic] Medal winner.

The history of the Lost Woman of San Nicholas Island is the base of "Island of the Blue Dolphins." O'Dell learned of the Indian girl he called Karana in 1956 while researching for his "Country of the Sun," a history of Southern California.

What is known of the lost woman can be told in a few words. Records of a ship's captain show that when the Indians left the island she leaped into the sea and returned because her brother was left behind.
She lived alone on the island of perpetual winds for 18 years after her brother was killed by wild dogs and was found by a Captain Nidever in 1853.

This historical fragment is elaborated by the author in a first-person narrative that firmly orients the reader. "The narrator is a person rather than male or female, but it was necessary that she not be a repetition of Dafoe's [Defoe's] "Robinson Crusoe," O'Dell said.
Inventive methods of survival were taken from a combination of coastal lore and the author's imagination.

Lamps were made by the girl from the dried bodies of little fish called "sai-sai"[sic]. These silver fish come swimming out of the sea in schools so thick you can almost walk on them "on nights when the moon shines full", [sic] says the girl.
This is an example of the sense of reality and time placement the adventure story gives. The girl could not call the fish grunion, as we know them, or the story would be out the window, said O'Dell.
She measured time by suns and moons, and first marked the seasons, then only the years, and at last made no record at all.
The girl avoids the easy label of "earthquake" by saying "the earth rose and fell like a great animal breathing."

The newspaperman, intensely conscious of his craft, has an effortless style in the story designed for both adults and children. "The book wrote itself," he said, "and this is the only time it has ever happened."

O'Dell has been book editor of the Los Angeles Mirror last fall after five or six years there. He is an authority on California history, and wrote "Hill of the Hawk," a regional novel. An uninteresting area is likely to be dismissed by O'Dell as "a 10,000 word topic."
Only Anglo Saxon words are used in the lyric prose of "Island of the Blue Dolphins." Good writing has been defined as talk written down and O'Dell's language sounds well read aloud.

There is a classic simplicity in this story of the sea. Few sea stories can afford to meander down unnecessary literary paths; something fundamental in the sea rejects unessentials. The object of speaking or writing should be to make something clear. If this does not happen, the story is pointless no matter how pretty the prose.
O'Dell's prize winning novel is as clear as the poster on the corner illuminated by innate good taste.

He visited the four by eight mile island (typically measured archaically as "leagues" in the book) about 30 years ago when with the technical department of Paramount Pictures which sought a location for Stevenson's "Ebb Tide." (San Nicholas Island was not chosen because of its lack of a harbor and landing dangers. The force of pounding waves and furious winds are expected to sweep the island back into the sea someday.)

The ways of coastal living and knowledge of plants and animals set down in a book by O'Dell were not gained from one. Great mounds of abalone shells shining in the sun were seen by the author when he was a boy watching the harvesting of the sea near Palos Verdes in the days before there were conservation laws. "You could chew on a dried abalone all day," he remembered.

The trays of dying meat destined for China covered 25 or 30 acres. The sights and sounds of these years led to Karana's fanciful scarecrow of abalone shells on the island. The shells were hung on poles. Their insides were bright, they caught the sun, and turned one way and another in the wind.

It is doubtful that anyone could grow too old to be delighted by the ingenuity of a whale rib fence to rebuff wild dogs. "I just wondered how one could be protected on an island with no wood," said O'Dell, "and this seemed logical."
"It was important to me to say the things in the book," said O'Dell, "we are in danger of losing the qualities of independence and self-reliance."

He speaks unhappily of an age of specialization when lawyers can speak only of torts, and admires his Julian neighbors who have practical, diversified knowledge. One neighbor has built a 5,000 square foot house by himself, including plastered ceilings and electrical wiring. Another invented a nasal spray.
"Writers should write, not talk," said the writer who doesn't complain of a lack of literary neighbors.

O'Dell, despite his admiration of self-reliance, would rather write than tend his 30-acre apple orchard or split wood although he does this occasionally. "Wood is $35 a cord, but can be split at $7 a cord," he remarked.

O'Dell has cancelled previous contracts to clear the decks for another two-level book. This one will concern a vain Spaniard who had five or six suits of armor and discovered California in 1540 two years before Cabrillo came. He was named Alar Con and called himself the "son of the sun," thereby impressing early California residents considerably.
The book[,] which won the Newberry [sic] Award[,] also is entered in the Hans Christian Anderson [Andersen] international contest. "Island of the Blue Dolphins" will be around a long time.
The highly regarded Newberry [sic] award insures the life of a book; the story's topic had wide general interest for all ages; and it is beautifully written.
